---
title: "Records vs mappings"
subtitle: "Aleo has two kinds of state. Picking the wrong one leaks privacy."
---

::: {.callout-note}
**Status:** Stub. Full chapter coming soon.
:::

## The question

You're modeling user balances on Aleo. Should each balance be a `record`
(private, UTXO-style) or an entry in a `mapping` (public, account-style)?

This is the **single most consequential design decision** in any Aleo dApp.
Pick wrong and your "private app" is fully transparent.

## What this chapter will cover

- The mental model: **records ≈ Bitcoin UTXOs**, **mappings ≈ Solidity mappings**
- Why Aleo has both (and why most chains pick only one)
- The consume-and-produce pattern: why passing a record into a function "spends" it
- Concrete examples from `credits.aleo` showing both:
  - `transfer_public(addr, u64)` — touches a public mapping
  - `transfer_private(input: credits, ...)` — consumes a record, produces new ones
- The 4 transfer-bridge patterns: public→public, private→private, public↔private

## Interactive viz planned

A side-by-side simulator: same dataset, two visualizations.

- **Left**: shown as a public mapping (you watch every entry update on every tx)
- **Right**: shown as private records (only the owner sees their own state)

Press "send 100 credits from Alice to Bob" and watch what each model leaks.

## Why this matters

Devs coming from EVM reflexively reach for `mapping`. Devs coming from Bitcoin
reflexively reach for `record`. Aleo's privacy story works only when you mix
them deliberately — public for what observers should see, private for what they
shouldn't.
